original_image_tags: Large black-and-white photograph of a dark, indistinct object in the sky. Caption below reads:
Newspaper clipping from "The Christian Science Monitor", Monday, May 2, 1966. Cartoon illustration of a large UFO hovering over a field. A man in a suit stands nearby. Caption reads:
Newspaper clipping from "Hong Kong Standard, May 14, 1966. Black-and-white photograph of a man, BRIAN POLLOCK, looking at a newspaper. Text reads:
Newspaper clipping from "Toronto, Canada Telegram, March 28, 1966". Headline reads:
Newspaper clipping from "Portland Oregon Journal, March 21, 1966". Headline reads:

elements.description: Newspaper front page layout ('Oakland Tribune', Friday, March 25, 1966). Main headline: 'Flying Saucers Are Poppin' Up All Over'.
Photograph in top right corner showing a small dark object against a lighter sky. Caption: 'UFO'S (?) FLASH ACROSS PRE-DAWN SKY. Time exposure taken by Michigan deputy sheriff.'
Photograph on the left side showing a silhouette of a man holding a camera or binoculars, standing near a vehicle, looking up. Caption: 'Flying swamp gas?'
Photograph in the center showing a cluster of light-colored, circular or blob-like shapes against a dark background. Caption: 'THESE ARE THE OBJECTS photographed by 16-year-old Brian Pollock...'.

Photograph in the bottom center showing a man in a suit and tie, looking towards the camera. Caption below mentions 'KENNETH ARNOLD, the Boise, Idaho, businessman who first reported seeing a flying saucer...'.
Photograph in the bottom right area showing a large sign on a building. Text on sign: 'BREWER Welcomes UFOs LANDING SITES AVAILABLE Signed... Barry Ivers MAYOR'.

Drawing in the center-right area showing a saucer-shaped object with a dome on top. Labels around it include 'LIGHT', 'LIGHT', 'LIGHT', 'SQUARE WINDOWS', and 'ALUMINUM'. Caption: 'POLICEMAN and five other persons who saw unidentified object... composite drawing of what they sighted.'

judge.reason: Description A and B describe completely different visual content, names, and captions (e.g., B quotes text about 'Brian Pollock' and 'Kenneth Arnold' while A mentions different newspapers and a different object description).
